column5: Use a token bucket instead of a sliding window so memory per client stays constant regardless of traffic
Store bucket state in a striped mutex map instead of a single global lock to remove lock contention under concurrency
Key rate limit buckets by API key rather than IP address, since IP addresses collide behind corporate NAT gateways
Refill tokens lazily on each request rather than with a background ticker, avoiding a goroutine per client
Return a Retry-After header computed from the next refill time so clients stop polling blindly after a 429
Cap burst size at 2x the sustained rate so one client can't starve the shared connection pool
Abandoned a Redis-backed shared counter: the network round trip added 40ms p99 latency to every request
Abandoned a sharded counter using atomic increments: it undercounted during the midnight UTC rollover window
Abandoned generating configs from a YAML file parsed at request time: parsing overhead added 3ms to the hot path
Abandoned a leaky bucket queue implementation: it required unbounded goroutine backlogs that risked OOMing the process
User requirement: handle at least 50,000 requests per second without adding external dependencies
User requirement: the limiter must work across multiple regions without a shared database
Discovered the load balancer strips the X-Forwarded-For header at the edge, so client identity can't come from it
Discovered the CI sandbox's clock skews by up to 2 seconds, breaking tests that assert exact refill timestamps
